description: One hand opens; a bird leaves. 'leiro' lets someone or something leave a present hold or containment. Arithmetic uses the same verb for subtraction and names the quantity that remains after a selected amount is released.
articulatory_notes: Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for voiced 'l', let air pass along its sides, and spread the lips for middle-height front 'e'. Keep the vowels separate as the tongue rises into stressed high-front 'i'. Make a tap or short trill with the tongue tip for 'r', then raise the back of the tongue and round the lips for middle-height back 'o'.
sound_symbolism: The clear hiatus between 'e' and stressed 'i' creates a small audible separation before final 'ro' rolls away. That contour can recall the instant when contact ends and motion continues.
usage_notes: The released person, thing, substance, or constraint is the direct object, and the event-noun rule supplies release. 'renoa' (liberate) removes a person or group from imposed bondage or control. Arithmetic uses '[starting quantity] nela [quantity released] leiro' followed by the remainder with 'kelai'; both quantities remain commensurable.

examples.phi: ta phoi nela ta shao leiro. wi shao kelai.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: In reflective practice, 'leiro' can describe loosening a grasp or attachment. The same word can also report an unwanted loss, so the surrounding sentence still matters.

semantic_domains.activity: ending a hold or containment so something can move away
semantic_domains.cognition: carrying arithmetic subtraction through an operation and remainder
semantic_domains.physical: letting a person, object, or substance leave restraint or containment
semantic_domains.quantity: removing a selected amount from a starting quantity
semantic_domains.wisdom: release considered as a practice of loosening attachment
